query: evidence on documentation of opioid dependence and methadone maintenance treatment in medical records

retrieved_docs.title: Vermont Controlled Substance Abuse, Diversion, Storage, Disposal, Monitoring, and Legal Issues -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Maintain Accurate Medical Records Regarding Opiate Prescriptions
retrieved_docs.content: All clinicians should maintain accurate, complete, and current medical records, including: Records of prescriptions for controlled substances Record instructions provided Detailed history, physical, monitoring, and reasons prescribed

retrieved_docs.title: Vermont Controlled Substance Abuse, Diversion, Storage, Disposal, Monitoring, and Legal Issues -- Treatment Planning -- Discontinuing Opioid Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Discontinuing opioid therapy should be based on a physician-patient discussion. Opioids should be discontinued when the pain has resolved, side effects develop, analgesia is inadequate, quality of life is not improved, deteriorating function, or there is evidence of aberrant medication use. Opioids should be tapered slowly, and an addiction specialist should manage withdrawal.

retrieved_docs.title: Vermont Controlled Substance Abuse, Diversion, Storage, Disposal, Monitoring, and Legal Issues -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Opioid therapy should begin as a trial for a pre-defined period, usually less than 30 days. Treatment goals should be established before the initiation of opioid therapy. These include the level of relief of pain, anxiety, depression, and the return of function while avoiding unnecessary opioid use. The plan should include therapy selection, progress measures, and additional consultations, evaluations, referrals, and therapies. The provider should:

retrieved_docs.title: West Virginia Opioid Prescribing for Chronic Pain While Avoiding Drug Diversion -- Pearls and Other Issues -- West Virginia Controlled Substance Monitoring Program
retrieved_docs.content: Sets opioid prescriptions limited to a three-day supply in all of the following cases Any prescription to a minor (>18 years of age) Adult patients seeking treatment in an ER setting for outpatient use A prescription issued by a dentist or an optometrist Limit all other Schedule II opioid prescriptions to a maximum 7-day supply at the lowest effective dose After issuing the initial prescription, after consultation with the patient, the prescriber may issue a subsequent prescription for an opioid if: the practitioner determines the prescription is necessary and appropriate to the patient's treatment needs Documents the rationale for the issuance of the subsequent prescription Determines that issuance of the subsequent prescription does not present an undue risk of abuse, addiction, or diversion and documents that determination If a third prescription for a Schedule II opioid is necessary, the patient should be referred to a chronic pain clinic If the patient remains a patient and the practitioner continues to prescribe an opioid for pain, the following steps are required: Review every three months the course of treatment, the etiology of the pain, the patient's progress toward treatment objectives, and document the results of that review Assess prior to every renewal to determine whether the patient is experiencing problems associated with physical and psychologic dependence and document the results of that assessment Periodically make reasonable efforts to either stop the use of the controlled substance, decrease the dosage, and/or try other drugs or treatment modalities and document with specificity the efforts undertaken Review the Controlled Substance Monitoring Database as required Exceptions Prescriptions for patients in active treatment for cancer, hospice care from a licensed hospice provider or palliative care provider, or residents of a long-term care facility, or to any medications that are being prescribed for use in the treatment of substance abuse or opioid dependence Patients being prescribed or ordered any medication in an inpatient setting at a hospital. The prescribing of non-opioid Schedule II controlled substances and opioid medications not classified as Schedule II controlled substances An existing provider-patient relationship established before January 1, 2018, where there is an established and current opioid treatment plan reflected in the patient's medical record

retrieved_docs.title: Opioid Addiction (Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Mainstreaming Addiction Treatment (MAT) Act
retrieved_docs.content: There are no longer any limits on the number of patients with OUD that a practitioner may treat with buprenorphine. Separate tracking of patients treated with buprenorphine or prescriptions written is no longer required.

retrieved_docs.title: Opioid Addiction (Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Mainstreaming Addiction Treatment (MAT) Act
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacy staff can now fill buprenorphine prescriptions using the prescribing authority's DEA number and does not need a DATA 2000 waiver from the prescriber. However, depending on the pharmacy, the dispensing software may still require the X-Waiver information in order to proceed. Practitioners are still required to comply with any applicable state limits regarding the treatment of patients with OUD.  Contact information for State Opioid Treatment Authorities can be found here: https://www.samhsa.gov/medicationassisted-treatment/sota.

retrieved_docs.title: Opioid Addiction (Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Mainstreaming Addiction Treatment (MAT) Act
retrieved_docs.content: As of December 2022, the MAT Act has eliminated the DATA-Waiver (X-Waiver) program. All DEA-registered practitioners with Schedule III authority may now prescribe buprenorphine for OUD in their practice if permitted by applicable state law, and SAMHSA encourages them to do so. Prescribers who were registered as DATA-Waiver prescribers will receive a new DEA registration certificate reflecting this change; no action is needed on the part of registrants.

retrieved_docs.title: Vermont Controlled Substance Abuse, Diversion, Storage, Disposal, Monitoring, and Legal Issues -- Evaluation -- Prescribing Opioids
retrieved_docs.content: Before prescribing opioids, complete a detailed patient history that includes the following: Indication requested for pain relief Location, nature, and intensity of pain Prior pain treatments and response Comorbid conditions Potential physical and psychological pain impact on function Family support, employment, and housing Leisure activities, mood, sleep, substance use, and work Emotional, physical, or sexual abuse

retrieved_docs.title: Vermont Controlled Substance Abuse, Diversion, Storage, Disposal, Monitoring, and Legal Issues -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who have developed opioid dependence secondary to pharmacological therapy should be offered treatment, possibly referral for addiction treatment or detoxification if indicated. The patient should be put on a medication weaning schedule or possibly medications for the treatment of opioid dependence.

retrieved_docs.title: Opioid Toxicity(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Mainstreaming Addiction Treatment (MAT) Act
retrieved_docs.content: There are no longer any limits on the number of patients with OUD that a practitioner may treat with buprenorphine. Separate tracking of patients treated with buprenorphine or prescriptions written is no longer required.

retrieved_docs.title: West Virginia Opioid Prescribing for Chronic Pain While Avoiding Drug Diversion -- Pearls and Other Issues -- West Virginia Controlled Substance Monitoring Program
retrieved_docs.content: Establishes a voluntary nonopioid advance directive form and patient education requirements before prescribing a Schedule II opioid.

retrieved_docs.title: Vermont Controlled Substance Abuse, Diversion, Storage, Disposal, Monitoring, and Legal Issues -- Evaluation -- VIGIL
retrieved_docs.content: Verification: Is this a responsible opioid user? Identification: Is the identity of this patient verifiable? Generalization: Do we agree on mutual responsibilities and expectations? Interpretation: Do I feel comfortable allowing this person to have controlled substances? Legalization: Am I acting legally and responsibly?

retrieved_docs.title: Kentucky KASPER and Controlled Substance Prescribing -- Treatment Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Reasons for opioid therapy change or discontinuation should be listed. Agreements can also include follow-up visits, monitoring, and safe storage and disposal of unused drugs. If the patient does not speak English, an interpreter should be used.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 187
retrieved_docs.content: specific form of drug use disorder; and maintenance therapy for opioid substances is linked to the management of opioid dependence and the drug withdrawal syndrome. Together, these entities and their parent‑child, member, and associative relationships provide a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and treating substance‑related health issues and their toxic, endocrine, and withdrawal sequelae.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2144
retrieved_docs.content: Clonidine hydrochloride is a pharmacologic agent that can be employed in the management of disorders associated with opioid use, such as opioid withdrawal symptoms and other opioid‑related conditions. By targeting the underlying physiological mechanisms of these opioid‑related disorders, clonidine hydrochloride helps alleviate withdrawal effects and supports patient stabilization during opioid cessation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1559
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic approach termed علاج المداومة للمواد الأفيونية (maintenance therapy for opioid substances) is classified as a member of متلازمة انسحاب المخدر (SMQ) (drug withdrawal syndrome), indicating that this opioid maintenance treatment is included within the broader syndrome grouping used to identify and manage drug withdrawal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 889
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine withdrawal is recognized as a diagnostic criterion for substance‑related disorders, indicating that the presence of withdrawal symptoms from cocaine contributes to the classification of these disorders. Substance‑related disorders are organized under the broader category of withdrawal and relapse effects, and they include the drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) as one of their members. The drug withdrawal syndrome (SMQ) also encompasses specific treatment approaches such as maintenance therapy for opioid substances, reflecting the role of opioid maintenance programs in managing withdrawal. Within the overarching framework of withdrawal and relapse effects, rebound tachycardia is identified as a specific physiological manifestation that can occur during withdrawal or after relapse. This interconnected structure highlights how withdrawal phenomena, diagnostic criteria, syndromic classifications, therapeutic strategies, and acute cardiovascular responses are linked in the clinical understanding and management of substance‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1392
retrieved_docs.content: Buprenorfin is classified as a naturally occurring alkaloid and is employed as a maintenance treatment for opioid substances (علاج المداومة للمواد الأفيونية), underscoring its role both as a naturally derived compound and as a pharmacologic agent used in the long‑term management of opioid dependence.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 510
retrieved_docs.content: Buprenorfin is identified as a naturally occurring alkaloid. It is employed in علاج المداومة للمواد الأفيونية (maintenance therapy for opioid substances), a therapeutic approach used to manage opioid dependence and support long‑term stabilization of patients. This maintenance therapy is categorized as a member of متلازمة انسحاب المخدر (SMQ) (the drug withdrawal syndrome grouping), reflecting its role in addressing the clinical manifestations of opioid withdrawal. Together, these relationships highlight buprenorfin’s pharmacologic classification, its therapeutic application in opioid maintenance treatment, and its inclusion within the broader framework of drug withdrawal syndrome management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: buprenorphine is described both as a naturally occurring alkaloid and as a plant‑derived agent product, while lofexidine and beta‑funaltrexamine function as narcotics antagonists. Together, these entities illustrate intersecting pharmacologic classifications across cardiovascular rhythm management, antihypertensive therapy, and opioid dependence treatment, providing clinicians with a structured understanding of drug classes, therapeutic indications, formulation options, and safety considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1241
retrieved_docs.content: Levomethadone and methadon are both classified as members of the drug category “Drugs used in opioid dependence,” indicating that they are therapeutic agents employed in the management of opioid dependence.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 448
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs used in opioid dependence comprise buprenorfin, levomethadone, and lofexidine, each classified as a member of this therapeutic group. Buprenorfin is identified as a naturally occurring alkaloid and is specifically employed for علاج المداومة للمواد الأفيونية (maintenance treatment of opioid dependence). This maintenance therapy is categorized within متلازمة انسحاب المخدر (SMQ), the drug‑withdrawal syndrome framework, highlighting its role in managing opioid withdrawal. Levomethadone and lofexidine, also members of the Drugs used in opioid dependence group, are utilized in similar clinical contexts to support patients with opioid dependence and to mitigate withdrawal symptoms. Together, these agents represent key pharmacologic options for the long‑term management and stabilization of individuals undergoing opioid dependence treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 35
retrieved_docs.content: substance‑abuse SMQ. The SMQ for drug withdrawal syndrome captures the constellation of symptoms that appear during opioid withdrawal or relapse, incorporates opioid maintenance therapy as a specific management strategy, and recognizes physiological manifestations such as rebound tachycardia that may arise during withdrawal or relapse. This integrated chemical, pharmacologic, diagnostic and therapeutic knowledge illustrates how heterocyclic and organic nitrogen chemistry underlies many agents used to manage dependence, withdrawal, intoxication and poisoning, and how these agents and clinical concepts are organized within a multilingual hierarchical structure of substance‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 57
retrieved_docs.content: concepts illustrate a structured network in which CNS‑active and psychotropic agents, various psychoactive substances (including hallucinogens), their dependence and withdrawal syndromes, adverse drug reactions, and therapeutic interventions (such as cholinergic antagonists and opioid maintenance therapy) are organized under the umbrella of substance‑related and addictive disorders. This hierarchy supports comprehensive diagnosis, risk assessment, and integrated patient care across the spectrum of substance use and addiction medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1690
retrieved_docs.content: Cocaine withdrawal is recognized as a diagnostic criterion within the broader category of substance‑related disorders, meaning that the presence of withdrawal symptoms after cessation of cocaine use helps clinicians identify and classify substance‑related disorders. This relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
retrieved_docs.content: Levomethadone and lofexidine are classified as drugs used in opioid dependence, and both are members of the therapeutic category of medications indicated for the management of opioid dependence. These agents are employed to alleviate withdrawal symptoms and support patients in recovery from opioid use disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Naltrexone hydrochloride is directly linked to opioid dependence (الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية), serving as a pharmacologic agent used to treat and prevent dependence on opioid compounds. By antagonizing opioid receptors, naltrexone hydrochloride reduces cravings and blocks the effects of opioids, highlighting its role in the management of opioid use disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 187
retrieved_docs.content: and misuse‑related conditions encompassed by substance‑related disorders. Maintenance therapy for opioid substances (“علاج المداومة للمواد الأفيونية”) is considered a component of the drug withdrawal syndrome (“متلازمة انسحاب المخدر (SMQ)”), highlighting its therapeutic relevance in the management of withdrawal. Finally, the withdrawal and relapse effects category (“تأثيرات الانسحاب والرجوع”) includes the specific manifestation “تسرع القلب الارتدادي” (rebound tachycardia), indicating a possible cardiovascular consequence of withdrawal or relapse. Together, these interrelated concepts delineate a comprehensive framework for understanding the classification, clinical features, and treatment strategies associated with substance‑related disorders across multiple languages and medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 253
retrieved_docs.content: concepts depict a comprehensive view of substance‑related disorders, encompassing diagnostic criteria (e.g., cocaine withdrawal), various substance‑induced psychiatric and behavioral states, hierarchical classifications across languages, specific syndromes such as drug withdrawal, therapeutic approaches like opioid maintenance, and physiological sequelae such as rebound tachycardia. This integrated framework aids clinicians in recognizing the full spectrum of substance‑related conditions, their diagnostic components, and associated treatment and complication pathways.
